segments.title: The Continental System and Britain's Baltic stake
segments.summary: The opening context links the 1807 expedition to Napoleon's Continental System and Britain's dependence on Baltic supplies and North Sea trade.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke sets the Second Battle of Copenhagen beside the 1801 action, then explains why the 1807 crisis mattered more than a dispute with Denmark alone. Britain treated Baltic stores and the trade routes around the North Sea as vital economic and naval resources. Denmark's apparent movement into Napoleon's Continental System therefore raised the prospect that a strategically located fleet and coastline could serve a hostile continental order.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts 1801 and 1807 and identifies the Continental System, Baltic stores, and North Sea trade as Britain's concern.

segments.title: Denmark's strategic bind and an unready fleet
segments.summary: Denmark faced pressure from Napoleonic France, but leaving its fleet concentrated at Copenhagen made it vulnerable to a Royal Navy attack.
segments.body: The video treats Denmark's alignment problem as a real strategic dilemma: refusing Napoleon while sharing a land frontier with him carried grave risks. Yet the Danish capital held an unready fleet and only a limited defending force, while Britain could send a superior naval force. Dr. Clarke's practical lesson is that a maritime state expecting British hostility must either prepare to defend its fleet or give it room to operate at sea.

evidence.note: The transcript describes Danish forces and fleet readiness, then weighs the danger of alignment with Napoleon against the danger from Britain.

segments.title: What it meant to “Copenhagen” an enemy
segments.summary: Dr. Clarke explains the episode's enduring shorthand: combining surprise with the seizure of an opponent's means of resistance.
segments.body: The Second Battle of Copenhagen gave rise, in the video's telling, to the verb “to Copenhagen”: to surprise an opponent and take everything significant from them. The 1807 operation differed from 1801 by pairing the naval force with substantial troops ashore. That combination makes the phrase a useful entry point into pre-emptive maritime coercion rather than a simple fleet battle.

evidence.note: The transcript defines the term and contrasts the 1807 use of ground forces with the first battle.

segments.title: Narrow waters, shore guns, and mortar ships
segments.summary: A tactical comparison with 1801 shows why Copenhagen's channels and fortifications should have favored a prepared defence—and how British mortar ships helped overcome it.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke stresses that forcing a fleet through narrow channels ought to expose it to concentrated fire from prepared shore positions. He then describes British mortar vessels, whose specialist guns were operated with Royal Artillery expertise as well as naval gunnery knowledge. Their bombardment helped suppress or push back defences so that Nelson's force could close in and capture Danish ships.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses defended channels, heated shot, mortar vessels, artillery specialists, and the approach that enabled Nelson's manoeuvre.

segments.title: Gambier and Cathcart's joint command team
segments.summary: The expedition's naval and military leadership reveals that Copenhagen was planned as a combined operation, not merely a Royal Navy raid.
segments.body: The video introduces Admiral James Gambier and General William Cathcart as the commanders chosen for the 1807 expedition. Gambier is presented as an experienced admiral whose later controversy at the Basque Roads should not obscure his professional judgement. The pairing matters because the coming operation needed coordinated sea power, troops, and political direction rather than an independent naval action.

evidence.note: The transcript names Gambier and Cathcart, discusses Gambier's experience, and introduces their roles in the second battle.

segments.title: Cathcart's army and the weight of the expedition
segments.summary: Cathcart's force included senior officers, the King's German Legion, artillery, engineers, and a large siege train—evidence of a major combined expedition.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke argues that Cathcart deserves greater attention for both his diplomatic work with Russia and his command responsibilities in 1807. He outlines a force that included the King's German Legion, Wellesley's reserve, other divisions, engineers, and large numbers of field and siege guns. The composition shows that British pressure on Copenhagen could be applied from land as well as from the sea.

evidence.note: The transcript covers Cathcart's wider career, the expedition's divisions, the King's German Legion, artillery, engineers, and the stated field and siege guns.

segments.title: Why Gambier chose a fleet of third rates
segments.summary: The fleet list explains why the Royal Navy relied chiefly on capable third rates rather than treating the expedition as a first-rate show of force.
segments.body: Gambier's initial force included the 98-gun Prince of Wales but was otherwise built around useful third rates, including 74-gun ships. Dr. Clarke emphasizes that this was not a weak force; it gathered solid ships and experienced captains while leaving scarce first rates available for other commitments. The choice reflects how a fleet commander balances prestige and firepower against the wider demands on a global navy.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies Prince of Wales, the third-rate-heavy force, several named ships, and Gambier's willingness to leave first rates for other operations.

segments.title: A second-rate flagship chosen for command
segments.summary: Gambier's choice of the second-rate Prince of Wales shows a commander valuing visibility and command organization over the status of a first-rate flagship.
segments.body: The video explains the trade-off in a second rate: considerable strength and height, but less graceful handling than longer first or third rates. Gambier could have demanded a first rate, yet he chose the higher masts of a second rate as a command advantage. He also used an unusual two-captain arrangement on the flagship, separating ship handling from flag-officer administration.

evidence.note: The transcript compares second rates with other rates, explains Gambier's preference for mast height, and describes the two-captain arrangement.

segments.title: Reinforcements, shallow waters, and naval diplomacy
segments.summary: Later reinforcements strengthened Gambier's force, but the campaign still favored versatile ships that could operate toward Copenhagen's shallower approaches and compel negotiation.
segments.body: Additional ships and a rear admiral joined the expedition, confirming the importance London attached to the operation. Even so, Dr. Clarke presents the preference for 74s and other third rates as an operational choice: they were strong enough for combat while more useful toward shallow waters than the largest ships. Before action began, the assembled force also functioned as naval diplomacy, intended to make Denmark reconsider before coercion became unavoidable.

evidence.note: The transcript lists arriving reinforcements, explains the utility of third rates and Prince of Wales, and describes the force as an attempt at naval diplomacy.
